data.title: Savara Says FDA Has No Plans for Advisory Meeting for Lung Disease Treatment Application
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan

Savara said the Food and Drug Administration does not plan to convene an advisory committee to review its biologics license application for a potential lung disease treatment.

The biopharmaceutical company said Friday the FDA's review of its application for Molbreevi is ongoing, targeting a Prescription Drug User Fee Act action date of Aug. 22.

The company also said it submitted a marketing authorization application to the European Medicines Agency, and its submission to the U.K. Medicines and Healthcare Products Regulatory Agency remains on track by the end of the first quarter.

Molbreevi was previously granted fast track, breakthrough therapy and orphan drug designations by the FDA. It aims to treat autoimmune pulmonary alveolar proteinosis, a rare lung disease.

Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com

data.title: Spring Valley Acquisition III reports $0 revenue and $2.36M net income; EPS not provided
data.description:
Spring Valley Acquisition III reported no operating revenues for the period and posted net income of $2.3593 million, driven primarily by interest income on Trust Account securities; per-share EPS was not provided in the filing. Operating activities produced an operating loss of $450,346 due to operating costs, while interest income of $2.8096 million largely offset those expenses. The company continues in a pre-revenue SPAC phase pending completion of its business combination.

Financial Highlights

  • Revenue: $0 (no operating revenues generated during the period)
  • Operating Income: -$450,346 (operating costs of $450,346)
  • Net Income: $2,359,300 (driven by interest income on Trust Account securities of $2,809,600)
  • Net Income Per Ordinary Share: Not reported in the filing

Business Highlights

  • SPAC Transaction: Entered a definitive Business Combination Agreement to merge with General Fusion, with the intent that Spring Valley Acquisition III will become General Fusion after closing.
  • Capital Deployment: $232.8 million placed in a trust from the IPO to fund the business combination and support future operations post-closing.
  • Operational Focus: Continued pre-revenue activities including organizational setup, IPO readiness, target identification, and preparations for post-closing operations.
  • Regulatory & Approvals: Closing is contingent on multiple approvals, including British Columbia court approval, SEC registration, Nasdaq listing approval, and filings under applicable Nuclear Safety Act requirements.
  • Financing Support & PIPE: An anchor PIPE and investor subscription secured approximately $107.6 million in units (10,556,367 units at $10.20) to support the closing and integration.

Original SEC Filing: Spring Valley Acquisition Corp. III [ SVAC ] - 10-K - Mar. 06, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: International Money Express 2025 10-K: $607.8M Revenue, $1.08 Diluted EPS
data.description:
International Money Express reported total revenues of $607.8 million for the fiscal year, down from the prior year primarily due to lower transaction volumes, and delivered diluted earnings per share of $1.08 as net income declined to $32.7 million. The results reflect lower operating income and higher operating expenses versus the prior year. Management highlighted growth in digital channels and certain international subsidiaries that partially offset weaker retail agent volumes.

Financial Highlights

  • Total revenues: $607.8 million (decrease vs. 2024, primarily due to lower transaction volume)
  • Operating income: $55.7 million (down from $95.0 million)
  • Net income: $32.7 million (decreased vs. $58.8 million in 2024)
  • Earnings per common share - Basic: $1.09 (down from $1.81)
  • Earnings per common share - Diluted: $1.08 (down from $1.79)

Business Highlights

  • Revenue trend and transaction activity: Revenues declined approximately 7.7% year-over-year to $607.8 million, driven by an 8.5% drop in transaction counts and a 2.2% decline in principal sent.
  • Channel mix: Growth in digital channels and European subsidiaries partially offset declines in retail agent volumes; the company noted increased RaaS (Remittance-as-a-Service) activity.
  • Product and brand initiatives: Expanded digital offerings and RaaS relationships, along with payroll card fee activity, contributed to higher other income and increased digital adoption.
  • Network and operational changes: Agent network expanded by 5.4% year-over-year.
  • Restructuring and cost actions: A restructuring completed in 2025 is expected to reduce annual costs by approximately $2.5 million beginning in Q2 2026.

Original SEC Filing: International Money Express, Inc. [ IMXI ] - 10-K - Mar. 06, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: BKV 10-K: $1,008.8M Revenue, $1.98 EPS
data.description:
BKV reported total revenues and other operating income of $1,008.8 million for the year, driven by higher commodity prices and volumes, and delivered net income attributable to BKV of $173.1 million (net income of $174.8 million). The company posted income from operations of $217.6 million and reported basic and diluted net income per common share attributable to BKV of $1.98. Results reflect higher realized commodity prices, derivative gains, and consolidation impacts from recent transactions.

Financial Highlights

  • Total revenues and other operating income: $1,008.8 million, significant year‑over‑year increase driven by commodity price and volume gains.
  • Income (loss) from operations: $217.6 million, an improvement from a prior-year loss reflecting higher realized prices and derivative gains.
  • Net income (loss): $174.8 million, a turnaround from the prior-year loss, benefiting from higher commodity realizations and derivative gains.
  • Net income attributable to BKV: $173.1 million, reflecting consolidation impacts and noncontrolling interest adjustments.
  • Net income per common share attributable to BKV: Basic $1.98; Diluted $1.98, improved versus prior-year negative EPS.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue growth & volumes: Natural gas revenues rose 75% year‑over‑year to $675 million, driven by higher prices and increased production (305.0 Bcfe).
  • Channel & business mix shift: Increased consolidation of joint ventures, including BKV‑BPP Power and Cotton Cove, and the Bedrock acquisition expanded Barnett assets and operations.
  • Operational milestones: Commenced CCUS sequestration in November 2023; two additional CCUS projects are expected to begin sequestration in early to mid‑2026.
  • Strategy momentum: Advancing a closed‑loop model across upstream, midstream, power, and CCUS to optimize costs and support growth in low‑carbon power solutions.
  • Cost & efficiency initiatives: The Pad of the Future program and integration activities increased near‑term activity while aiming to improve long‑term operating efficiency.

Original SEC Filing: BKV Corp [ BKV ] - 10-K - Mar. 06, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: Shrinking Labor Force, Surging Oil Prices Drive Stocks to Worst Week Since April — WSJ
data.description:
By Vicky Ge Huang

A disappointing report on the U.S. labor market, a spike in oil prices and the specter of a protracted Middle East war converged at the end of a turbulent week, fueling fears of a stagflationary spiral that threatens to derail the U.S. economy.

The U.S. lost 92,000 jobs in February, well below January's gain of 126,000 and far worse than the 50,000 jobs expected by economists. The unemployment rate ticked higher, to 4.4%.

The weak jobs report arrived on Friday, as the widening war in the Middle East propelled global oil prices past $90 a barrel. The Strait of Hormuz, where around a fifth of global oil supply typically flows through every day, is effectively shut. Kuwait has begun cutting production at some oil fields after running out of room to store its bottled-up crude.

Both U.S. and international crude benchmarks notched their largest one-week gains on record. The West Texas Intermediate benchmark rose 12% Friday to $90.90 a barrel, its biggest daily jump since 2020. For the week, the U.S. oil benchmark surged 36%. Brent-crude futures gained 8.5% to $92.69 a barrel, up a record 27% for the week.

A cooling labor market coupled with surging energy costs can be a recipe for stagflation, a scenario that puts the Federal Reserve in a difficult position, investors say. The central bank is expected to hold rates steady at its March meeting. And traders have pared back their expectations on rate cuts this year.

"It literally sets up a perfect storm," said Ken Mahoney, chief executive of Mahoney Asset Management. "Here we are, just two months into the year, getting hit from all sides."

On Friday, investor anxiety over the Iran conflict intensified. The Dow Jones Industrial Average lost 453 points, or 0.9%, dragging the blue-chip index 3% lower for the week in its steepest retreat since April. The S&P 500 fell 1.3%, bringing its weekly loss to 2%. The tech-focused Nasdaq decreased 1.6%, capping a 1.2% weekly decline.

Investors already have plenty of things to worry about. Concerns over the disruption of artificial intelligence have weighed on everything from software to insurance providers. Last week, fintech firm Block laid off 40% of its employees, citing AI tools as the reason for the cuts.

More recently, signs of stress are showing up in the private-credit market where major firms are pausing redemptions and writing down loans. On Friday, BlackRock shares tumbled 7.2% after the asset manager limited withdrawals from one of its flagship private-credit funds for the first time.

Treasury yields logged hefty weekly gains despite ending the Friday session lower. The yield on the 10-year Treasury note, which influences borrowing costs across the economy, settled at 4.131%. The yield on the 2-year Treasury note, which reflects investor expectations on near-term interest rates, edged down to 3.554%.

Some investors remain optimistic that the Middle East conflict will be short-lived. Heading into the midterms, the administration has strong political incentives to bring energy costs down, said Robert Edwards, chief investment officer at Edwards Asset Management.

"We are telling clients to keep cool, fill your tank right now before it gets higher, drive a little less and look forward to a 7700 S&P by year-end," he said.

Mahoney said he has been buying the dip for his clients over the past week, snapping up shares of Microsoft, Nvidia and Caterpillar. He cautioned against betting on a continued market slide, pointing to the president's track record of sudden policy reversals.

"The President has this tendency to TACO," he said, referring to Trump's tendency to announce tough policies only to backtrack on those announcements later. "I'm sure he's getting an earful right now of oil prices and people getting really concerned again."

Write to Vicky Ge Huang at vicky.huang@wsj.com

data.title: HF Sinclair Corp Files 8K - Director, Officer or Compensation Filing >DINO
data.description:
HF Sinclair Corp (DINO) filed a Form 8K - Director, Officer or Compensation Filing - with the U.S Securities and Exchange Commission on March 06, 2026.

As previously announced in its Annual Report on Form 10-K filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission on February 27, 2026 (the "Form 10-K"), HF Sinclair Corporation (the "Company") appointed Mr. Vivek Garg, age 52, to the position of acting Chief Financial Officer ("Acting CFO") on February 24, 2026. Mr. Garg currently serves as the Company's Vice President, Chief Accounting Officer and Controller, and he will continue to hold those positions as he serves as Acting CFO.

The Company previously disclosed Mr. Garg's biographical information required by Item 401(e) of Regulation S-K in its Form 10-K.

There are no arrangements or understandings between Mr. Garg and any other person pursuant to which Mr. Garg was elected to serve as the Company's principal financial officer on a temporary basis. Mr. Garg does not have any family relationship with any director or executive officer of the Company, or any person nominated or chosen by the Company to become a director or executive officer. There are no transactions in which Mr. Garg has an interest requiring disclosure under Item 404(a) of Regulation S-K.

In addition to the compensation that he is receiving in his current role, the Compensation Committee of the Board of Directors of the Company approved the following additional compensation arrangements to be provided to Mr. Garg for his service as Acting CFO: (1) a monthly cash stipend in the amount of $25,000, commencing March 1, 2026, prorated based on his service during any partial month as Acting CFO, and (2) a one-time grant of restricted stock units, the grant date value of which shall equal $375,000 (the "RSU Grant"). Subject to his continued employment with the Company, fifty percent (50%) of the RSU Grant will vest on December 1, 2026, and fifty percent (50%) will vest on December 1, 2027, although the award will receive pro-rata vesting in the event of Mr. Garg's death or disability prior to the applicable vesting date. The RSU Grant will be further governed by the HF Sinclair Corporation Amended and Restated 2020 Long Term Incentive Plan and an individual restricted stock unit award agreement to be entered into between Mr. Garg and the Company, a form of which has previously been filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission by the Company. Mr. Garg is already a party to an individual Change in Control Agreement and Indemnification Agreement with the Company, and he will continue to be eligible to participate in all health, welfare and retirement plans generally available to all employees, including the Company's nonqualified deferred compensation plan. Mr. Garg will also continue to participate in the Company's annual bonus compensation program on the same terms, including the same target amount and performance measures, as were applicable to him prior to his appointment as Acting CFO.

The full text of this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1915657/000162828026015670/dino-20260306.htm

Any exhibits and associated documents for this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1915657/000162828026015670/0001628280-26-015670-index.htm

Public companies must file a Form 8-K, or current report, with the SEC generally within four days of any event that could materially affect a company's financial position or the value of its shares.

data.title: Omada Health 10-K: Revenue $260.2M, EPS $(0.35)
data.description:
Omada Health reported full-year revenue of $260.2 million with earnings per share of $(0.35), reflecting strong top-line growth driven by services expansion and a larger membership base while still delivering a modest net loss. Revenue rose 53% year-over-year as operating losses and net loss narrowed substantially versus the prior year. The company attributes margin expansion to Care Team efficiencies and continued investment in its care platform and programs.

Financial Highlights

  • Total revenue: $260.2 million, up 53% versus 2024 (Services $241.0 million; Hardware $19.2 million).
  • Gross profit: $170.9 million (65.7% margin), expanded 5.1 percentage points driven by Care Team efficiencies.
  • Operating loss: $(11.97) million, improved from $(43.65) million in 2024.
  • Net loss and comprehensive loss: $(12.78) million, improved versus $(47.14) million in 2024.
  • Net loss per share - basic and diluted: $(0.35); weighted-average shares outstanding: 36.639 million.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue growth drivers: Services and total revenue grew 53% year-over-year to $260.2 million, driven by a 52% rise in average members and higher fees per member.
  • Channel and customer mix: Continued reliance on major health plan and PBM partners, with the top five customers representing 77% of revenue, while expanding multi-channel go-to-market efforts.
  • Membership scale and engagement: Total members reached 886,000, up from 572,000, with emphasis on enrollment best practices and multi-program adoption to improve engagement.
  • Product and care expansion: Broadened offerings to include cardiometabolic, musculoskeletal, GLP-1 care tracks and behavioral health; began prescribing GLP-1s to support clinical care.
  • Operational efficiency and technology: Gross margin expansion attributed to Care Team efficiency initiatives and platform tools; continued investment in the Care Team Platform to support scale and clinical delivery.

Original SEC Filing: Omada Health, Inc. [ OMDA ] - 10-K - Mar. 06, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: Krispy Kreme 10-K: $1.52B Revenue, $(3.04) EPS
data.description:
Krispy Kreme reported total net revenues of $1,522.6 million for the year, down 8.6% from the prior year, and recorded a net loss per diluted share of $(3.04) driven by significant impairment charges. The company posted an operating loss of $(469.3) million, which included $432.4 million of goodwill and other asset impairments, and a net loss attributable to the company of $(515.8) million. Adjusted metrics show adjusted EBITDA of $140.3 million and adjusted EBIT of $34.5 million, both declining year over year.

Financial Highlights

  • Total net revenues: $1,522.6 million, down 8.6% vs prior year (organic revenue decline of 1.3%).
  • Operating (loss)/income: $(469.3) million; results were impacted by $432.4 million of goodwill and other asset impairments.
  • Net (loss)/income: $(523.8) million, compared with $3.8 million income in the prior year.
  • Net (loss)/income attributable to Krispy Kreme: $(515.8) million.
  • Net (loss)/income per share — Common stock - Diluted: $(3.04), prior year $0.02.
  • Adjusted EBITDA: $140.3 million, down 27.5% (used by management to assess operating performance).
  • Adjusted EBIT: $34.5 million, down 61.8% (used to assess segment performance).

Business Highlights

  • Revenue trend: Net revenues declined due to lower shop transactions and strategic closures; organic revenue fell 1.3%.
  • Channel shift: Growth in digital channels and third‑party delivery; the company added approximately 1,100 profitable delivery doors while closing about 1,400 underperforming doors.
  • Brand & innovation: Strong momentum from seasonal and specialty products, driving customer frequency and media attention across global markets.
  • Operational efficiency initiatives: A turnaround plan emphasizing refranchising, reduced capital expenditures (capex down 18.9%), outsourcing U.S. logistics to third‑party logistics providers, and hub optimization to improve margins.
  • Major milestones: Completed divestiture of Insomnia Cookies, sale of Japan business, and opened new international franchise markets such as Uzbekistan.

Original SEC Filing: Krispy Kreme, Inc. [ DNUT ] - 10-K - Mar. 06, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: FIRSTSUN Capital Bancorp 2025: $317.4M NII, $3.47 EPS on $97.9M Net Income
data.description:
FIRSTSUN reported strong 2025 results with net income of $97.9 million, or $3.47 diluted EPS, driven by a $20.5 million increase in net interest income to $317.4 million and higher mortgage banking and treasury management revenue. Total noninterest income rose to $101.9 million, supporting income before taxes of $122.9 million. The company posted a net interest margin of 4.10% (4.16% FTE) and an efficiency ratio of 64.82% (64.17% adjusted).

Financial Highlights

  • Net interest income: $317.4 million (↑ $20.5M vs 2024); net interest margin 4.10% (FTE 4.16%).
  • Total noninterest income: $101.9 million (↑ $12.1M vs 2024), driven by mortgage banking and treasury management.
  • Income before income taxes: $122.9 million (↑ $27.8M vs 2024).
  • Net income: $97.9 million; Diluted EPS: $3.47. (Adjusted net income $100.5M; adjusted diluted EPS $3.56.)
  • Efficiency ratio: 64.82% (adjusted 64.17%).

Business Highlights

  • Mortgage platform expansion: Mortgage origination volumes increased with loans for sale of $1.4 billion and a servicing portfolio that grew to $6.3 billion, bolstering mortgage banking income.
  • Deposit and funding trends: Core deposits grew approximately 6.6%, with notable growth in treasury management and money-market balances supporting loan-funded growth.
  • Workforce and capacity: Headcount increased to 1,177 to expand commercial & industrial lending and mortgage origination teams, contributing to higher salary and benefit expenses.
  • Strategic transaction: Pending merger with First Foundation to expand presence across California and Texas and add operations in Florida, Nevada and Hawaii; expected close in Q2 2026.

Original SEC Filing: FIRSTSUN CAPITAL BANCORP [ FSUN ] - 10-K - Mar. 06, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: Equity Bancshares 2025 10-K: $330.8M Interest Income, $1.23 Diluted EPS
data.description:
Equity Bancshares reported total interest and dividend income of $330.8 million for the year ended December 31, 2025, driven primarily by loan and securities yields, while diluted earnings per share were $1.23. Despite higher net interest income supporting core revenue, operating income before taxes was $26.4 million and net income allocable to common stockholders was $22.7 million, reflecting elevated operating costs and integration expenses tied to recent acquisitions. The results reflect loan growth and investment repositioning alongside merger-related costs that weighed on profitability versus the prior year.

Financial Highlights

  • Interest and dividend income: $330.8 million (total interest and dividend income)
  • Net interest income: $226.1 million
  • Operating income (Income (loss) before income taxes): $26.4 million
  • Net income allocable to common stockholders: $22.7 million
  • Diluted earnings per share: $1.23

Business Highlights

  • Revenue trend and drivers: Net interest income rose materially in 2025 driven by loan growth and investment repositioning, which underpinned core revenue despite a year-over-year decline in net income.
  • Loan growth & channel shift: Loans increased approximately 20% year-over-year with strategic emphasis on commercial loan expansion in metropolitan markets; the acquisition of NBC added scale and deposit capabilities.
  • Product & deposit mix: Shift toward higher-yield commercial real estate and commercial & industrial loans, with increased utilization of non-interest-bearing core deposits and ICS/CDARS programs to manage funding and liquidity.
  • Operational costs & integration: Operating expenses were elevated due to merger integration, staffing increases and conversion costs tied to recent acquisition activity and market expansion.
  • Brand & footprint: Continued expansion through M&A—23 acquisitions completed since 2003—resulting in 77 branches across four states with strategic growth in Kansas City, Tulsa, Oklahoma City and Wichita.

Original SEC Filing: EQUITY BANCSHARES INC [ EQBK ] - 10-K - Mar. 06, 2026

Disclaimer

data.title: Donegal 10-K: $978.0M Revenue, $2.18 Diluted EPS
data.description:
Donegal reported total revenues of $978.0 million for the year, driven primarily by $921.2 million of net premiums earned and $52.6 million of net investment income, and delivered diluted earnings of $2.18 per Class A share. The company recorded operating income (income before income tax expense) of $97.6 million and net income of $79.34 million after $18.25 million of income tax expense. Results reflect improved underwriting performance alongside ongoing operational investments and portfolio adjustments.

Financial Highlights

  • Total revenues: $978.0 million (includes net premiums earned $921.2M and net investment income $52.6M)
  • Operating income: $97.6 million (reported as income before income tax expense)
  • Net income: $79.34 million (after $18.25 million income tax expense)
  • Diluted earnings per common share: Class A $2.18 per share; Class B diluted EPS $2.01

Business Highlights

  • Revenue & premium trend: Net premiums written declined 4.0% in 2025 to $904.8 million; commercial lines grew 2.9% while personal lines declined 13.6% due to planned attrition strategies.
  • Underwriting performance: Combined ratio improved to 95.4% in 2025 from 98.6% in 2024, driven by a lower loss ratio and favorable prior-year reserve development.
  • Distribution & channel mix: Business continues to be sold exclusively through independent agents, with strategic emphasis on growing commercial products and de-emphasizing select personal lines.
  • Operational actions & projects: Ongoing systems modernization efforts contributed allocated costs (approximately 1.2 points of the expense ratio) and the farm line was exited under a renewal rights agreement as part of a planned 2026 exit.
  • Claims & loss management: Management highlighted favorable prior-year reserve development ($10.3 million in 2025) while noting attention to rising claim severity, longer settlement durations, and climate-related weather risks.

Original SEC Filing: DONEGAL GROUP INC [ DGICA ] - 10-K - Mar. 06, 2026

Disclaimer

data.title: The LNG Trade Has Gone Wild. What to Expect Next. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Avi Salzman

The disruption the Iran war has done to oil markets is bad enough. The dislocation in the market for liquefied natural gas — the backbone of Europe and Asia's electricity and heating supplies — has the potential to be even worse.

Liquefied natural gas, or LNG, depends heavily on Middle Eastern suppliers, who freeze and compress the gas and then ship it throughout the world. Right now, the most important suppliers can't get their products to customers.

Qatar, which accounts for about 20% of global supply, shut down its main LNG plant on Monday after it was hit by missiles from Iran. QatarEnergy, the state-run company, declared force majeure to get out of its contracts to supply LNG. Even if Qatar's plant were back up and running, there's no way to get energy products out of the Persian Gulf. The Strait of Hormuz, which takes products from the Gulf to the Arabian Sea and then mostly to Asia, is effectively blocked.

The price of LNG in Europe shot up more than 40% on Monday alone, and has stayed elevated both there and in Asia. The disruption has been a boon to U.S. exporters of LNG, including the two largest players, Cheniere Energy and Venture Global. Cheniere stock is up 9% since the attacks, and Venture has risen 30%.

Normally, it makes sense to avoid buying energy stocks in the midst of a crisis like this. They've already risen, and the end of the conflict will eventually cause prices to fall. The coming weeks will be full of volatility, with dips, pops, and more dips in the stocks.

But the war has also highlighted the long-term benefits of owning stocks like these. The chaos in the energy sector has made it clear, as did Russia's war in Ukraine, that American LNG plays an increasingly important role in keeping the lights on in Asia and Europe in times of crisis. Such disruptions can no longer be easily dismissed as one-off issues, says Ira Joseph, a researcher at the Center on Global Energy Policy at Columbia University. "We've had two once-in-a-generation massive gas supply disruptions in the past four years," he says.

While investors tend to value stocks based on their repeatable cash flows, they shouldn't overlook the windfall that's about to come in from near-term sales, which can be used to pay off debt or pay dividends. "Whoever is selling the LNG is going to make so much money," Joseph says. "It's the kind of money they weren't expecting to make in a decade."

The war could also affect the long-term decisions that LNG buyers make. Qatar sells its LNG through long-term contracts with end buyers, but its reliance on Hormuz to transport its products means that buyers may think twice before signing new contracts. "If you're building a power plant and want guaranteed gas [for 20 years], you'd have to be a lunatic to sign with Qatar Energy and think that is your best option," said Henry Hoffman, portfolio co-manager of the Catalyst Energy Infrastructure fund, which owns shares of U.S. suppliers like Cheniere, Venture Global, and NextDecade.

Venture, which is selling cargoes from a partially built plant in Louisiana that isn't expected to enter full contracted status until the fourth quarter, has particularly large exposure to the spot LNG market. Hoffman thinks the stock could get back to its IPO price of $25 this year, double its current price, and potentially rise much more. "It's on discount," he said. "That's a great short-term play."

Especially with the need for LNG at a premium.

Write to Avi Salzman at avi.salzman@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: American Rebel Holdings Files 8K - Unregistered Equity Sales >AREB
data.description:
American Rebel Holdings Inc. (AREB) filed a Form 8K - Unregistered Sales of Equity - with the U.S Securities and Exchange Commission on March 06, 2026.

On February 25, 2026, the Company issued Streeterville 2,450,000 shares of common stock pursuant to the exchanges set forth in Item 1.01 above.

All of the above-described issuances (if any) were exempt from registration pursuant to Section 4(a)(2), and/or Regulation D of the Securities Act as transactions not involving a public offering. With respect to each transaction listed above, no general solicitation was made by either the Company or any person acting on its behalf. All such securities issued pursuant to such exemptions are restricted securities as defined in Rule 144(a)(3) promulgated under the Securities Act, appropriate legends have been placed on the documents evidencing the securities, and may not be offered or sold absent registration or pursuant to an exemption therefrom.

The full text of this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1648087/000149315226009159/form8-k.htm

Any exhibits and associated documents for this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1648087/000149315226009159/0001493152-26-009159-index.htm

Public companies must file a Form 8-K, or current report, with the SEC generally within four days of any event that could materially affect a company's financial position or the value of its shares.

data.title: American Rebel Holdings Files 8K - Entry Into Definitive Agreement >AREB
data.description:
American Rebel Holdings Inc. (AREB) filed a Form 8K - Entry Into a Definitive Agreement - with the U.S Securities and Exchange Commission on March 06, 2026.

Streeterville Capital Funds Release

As previously disclosed, on June 26, 2025, the Company entered into a note purchase agreement with Streeterville Capital, LLC ("Streeterville") pursuant to which the Company issued and sold to Streeterville a secured promissory note in the original principal amount of $5,470,000. On the Closing Date, Streeterville paid $375,000.00 to the Company and $4,625,000 was sent to an account at Lakeside Bank owned by the Company's newly formed wholly-owned subsidiary, ARH Sub, LLC, a Utah limited liability company, to be held pursuant to the Deposit Account Control Agreement ("DACA"). On February 25, 2026, Streeterville and ARH Sub sent joint instructions to Lakeside Bank to release $250,000 from the DACA to the Company.

Streeterville Exchange Agreements

On February 25, 2026, the Company entered into five Exchange Agreements (the "Exchanges") with Streeterville. The Company previously issued to Streeterville 2,000 shares of Series E Preferred Stock pursuant to that certain Note Purchase Agreement entered into as of August 22, 2025. Pursuant to the Exchanges, the Company and Streeterville agreed to exchange and convert 490 shares of Series E Preferred Stock for 2,450,000 shares of common stock.

The form of Exchanges were identical for each exchange except for the conversion dollar amounts and number of shares converted thereunder.

The foregoing descriptions of the Exchanges are not a complete description of all of the parties' rights and obligations under the Exchanges, and are qualified in its entirety by reference to the Form Exchange Agreement, a copy of which is filed hereto as Exhibit 10.1.

The full text of this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1648087/000149315226009159/form8-k.htm

Any exhibits and associated documents for this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1648087/000149315226009159/0001493152-26-009159-index.htm

Public companies must file a Form 8-K, or current report, with the SEC generally within four days of any event that could materially affect a company's financial position or the value of its shares.

data.title: Salesforce Stock Might Not Be an AI Loser After All — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Adam Levine

Software's Surprise. Business customers have been slow to adopt artificial-intelligence tools in a systematic way, but some of that conservatism is beginning to fade. At Salesforce — one of the few software companies willing to open up about its AI business — core AI revenue is small but rising quickly.

Salesforce's AI momentum could come as a surprise to investors, who have been dumping software stocks for much of the year. The iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF is down 23% on the year. A narrative has arisen that AI will badly disrupt software, and that some of these companies, even ones that are thriving now, won't make it out alive.

AI agents are the focus of the worries. Agents are software that can use an AI language model to accomplish a complex series of tasks in much the same way a human worker would. They are still at an early stage, but, projecting forward, one can imagine a world in which a large portion of knowledge work is being done by machines, not people. And these machines may not need software in the way we think of it today.

The two primary sources of worries come from AI start-ups OpenAI and Anthropic, especially the latter. ChatGPT, OpenAI's general purpose chatbot, had a head start and is more popular with consumers, so Anthropic has focused its attention on software developers and business customers. Its two agentic products for those markets are called Claude Code and Claude Cowork.

Last month, Anthropic released Cowork tools for specific enterprise functions like finance and legal. Software stocks tumbled in the wake of those launches.

But neither company has experience in selling to enterprises, and we can infer from some of their statements that it's slower going than they'd like.

"The limiting factor for seeing value from AI in enterprises isn't model intelligence, it's how agents are built and run in their organizations," began a Feb. 23 press release from OpenAI announcing a new partnership with IT consultants. OpenAI needs their expertise to sell and implement AI in enterprise markets.

Anthropic was even more blunt in a business-focused presentation last week. "2025 was meant to be the year where AI agents transformed the enterprise. But the hype turned out to be mostly premature. Many pilots started and many failed," Kate Jensen, Anthropic's head of Americas, said in a livestream. "There was a growing sense that the technology was moving faster than the ability to actually deploy it well. It wasn't a failure of effort, it was a failure of approach."

It turns out the approach can't simply cut out existing software makers and their tightknit relationships with business customers.

As OpenAI and Anthropic have indicated, we are still near the beginning of a process described by economists like Erik Brynjolfsson, who have outlined the sometimes halting progress of new technologies. They have to be accompanied by new business processes, new worker skills, and new co-inventions.

Evidence from the biweekly U.S. Census Bureau's Business Trends and Outlook Survey shows that AI uptake is still low. An average of about 18% of U.S. businesses were using AI in the four 2026 surveys. But that rises to 32% for firms with 250 or more employees.

These numbers have been rising slowly since the Census Bureau began asking questions about AI in 2023.

During the cloud disruption two decades ago, many incumbent enterprise software makers looked down on a host of new browser-based applications. This time around, software companies are taking the threat seriously. Like many of its competitors, Salesforce is trying to sell agents to its customers, and it's starting to see rapid growth here.

Salesforce began to report annual recurring revenue for its agent software, Agentforce, in the second fiscal quarter, which ended in July. Back then it was $440 million, only about 1% of annual revenue. But when Salesforce reported its fourth quarter last week, Agentforce recurring revenue had jumped to $800 million, an 82% rise in six months.

This is still a relatively small number but Salesforce's agent trajectory is an important metric as enterprise AI adoption creeps higher. Since Salesforce is one of the few public software companies willing to put a number on AI revenue, it's also a barometer for enterprise adoption more generally.

Salesforce stock is down 25% this year.

Write to Adam Levine at adam.levine@barrons.com

This Week in Barron's Tech

  • Nvidia's Earnings Can't Buy a Rally, but Anthropic Just Threw Software a Lifeline
  • Amazon Leads Record $110 Billion OpenAI Funding Round. AWS Is Why.
  • MongoDB Stock Tumbles 21%. Why Analysts Still Say Buy.
  • Apple Launches New Laptops and Chips. AI Power Is a Focus.
  • Anthropic's Claude Holds Top Spot in App Store Following U.S. Government Ban

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: US court voids New Jersey's tougher standard for reverse bias lawsuits
data.description:
Key points:
  • 3rd Circuit invalidates New Jersey’s higher bar for reverse discrimination claims
  • Court revives lawsuit by white New Jersey police official over promotion decision
  • Ruling follows Supreme Court rejection of heightened proof standard

By Daniel Wiessner

A U.S. appeals court on Friday ruled that New Jersey's higher bar for reverse discrimination lawsuits by members of majority groups, such as white people and men, is invalid after the U.S. Supreme Court rejected an identical test under federal law.

A unanimous three-judge panel of the 3rd U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals revived a lawsuit by Christopher Massey, a white deputy police chief in Bergenfield, New Jersey, claiming a decision to promote an Arab-Muslim officer to chief instead of him was motivated by race and religion.

The Supreme Court in a unanimous June decision in Ames v. Ohio Department of Youth Services said a rule adopted by some federal appeals courts imposing a higher burden of proof in reverse discrimination cases was not allowed by Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964. Those courts had typically required plaintiffs to show "background circumstances," such as that a member of a minority group made an employment decision or that the employer had a pattern of discriminating against majority-group employees.

While the Philadelphia-based 3rd Circuit had previously rejected that requirement, the New Jersey Supreme Court in a 1990 case adopted it under a state law modeled after Title VII.

But the 3rd Circuit on Friday said the U.S. Supreme Court in Ames had made clear that Title VII was designed to eradicate all workplace discrimination and not impose special requirements on members of majority groups.

"New Jersey’s legislature did the same thing by adopting language that is, as relevant here, a mirror image," wrote Circuit Judge Emil Bove, who was appointed by Republican President Donald Trump after working as his personal lawyer.

The decision is among the first penned by Bove since he was confirmed in July over fierce opposition by Democrats who said his loyalty to Trump should disqualify him from the bench. Bove’s confirmation restored a majority of Republican appointees on the 3rd Circuit, which hears cases from New Jersey, Delaware and Pennsylvania.

Lawyers for Bergenfield, a borough of nearly 30,000 people in the northern reaches of New Jersey, did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

Dylan Hastings, a lawyer for Massey, said the decision affirms that race discrimination laws apply to everyone equally, regardless of their race.

“As can be gleaned from the decision, there is significant evidence that the promotional decision was indeed influenced by race, and we look forward to proving that at trial," Hastings said in an email.

DEI UNDER FIRE

The Trump administration has moved aggressively to eliminate diversity, equity and inclusion policies from the federal government, private sector and higher education. Trump and his supporters say that DEI programs erode merit-based decision-making and discriminate against majority groups.

The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission, which enforces Title VII, has targeted employer DEI policies and recently filed its first lawsuit of Trump's term involving a diversity initiative.

The Supreme Court ruling in Ames was seen as a boon to plaintiffs who sue employers over their treatment of white people or men and challenge DEI policies. It followed on a 2023 decision by the high court striking down race-conscious college admission policies, which has since been quoted in scores of lawsuits for the proposition that "eliminating racial discrimination means eliminating all of it.”

Massey, the deputy police chief, claims in a 2020 lawsuit that he was denied the promotion to chief because a committee making the decision believed he did not reflect the area's large Arab population. Massey says he was more qualified than the other candidate, who had been a captain, and that town officials told him the decision had been about race.

U.S. District Judge Jamel Semper dismissed the case in 2024, finding Massey had not shown any background circumstances supporting his claim as required by state law.

The 3rd Circuit on Friday reversed, saying that without the heightened burden of proof, Massey had made out an initial case of discrimination and the case could proceed. Bove said there was "more than enough" evidence to support an inference that officials factored in the candidates' race and religion.

The panel included Circuit Judges Michael Chagares and Anthony Scirica, who were appointed by other Republican presidents.

The case is Massey v. Borough of Bergenfield, 3rd U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals, No. 24-2761.

For Massey: Dylan Hastings of Williams Cedar

For the defendants: John Shahdanian and Zachary Poreman of Trenk Isabel Siddiqi & Shahdanian

Read more:

US Supreme Court makes 'reverse' discrimination suits easier

Trump-allied judge draws scrutiny for rally appearance

US appeals court rejects challenge to Trump's efforts to ban DEI

US agency sues Coca-Cola bottler over employee event that excluded men

US Supreme Court rejects affirmative action in university admissions

data.title: Helmerich & Payne Files 8K - Director, Officer or Compensation Filing >HP
data.description:
Helmerich & Payne Inc. (HP) filed a Form 8K - Director, Officer or Compensation Filing - with the U.S Securities and Exchange Commission on March 06, 2026.

At the 2026 Annual Meeting of Stockholders of Helmerich & Payne, Inc. (the "Company") held on March 4, 2026 (the "2026 Annual Meeting"), the Company's stockholders approved the Helmerich & Payne, Inc. Amended & Restated 2024 Omnibus Incentive Plan (the "A&R 2024 Plan"). The A&R 2024 Plan is a stock and cash-based incentive plan and includes provisions by which the Company may grant selected officers, employees, consultants, and non-employee directors stock options, share appreciation rights, restricted shares and restricted share units, share bonuses, other share-based awards, and cash awards. The A&R 2024 Plan is more fully described in the Company's Definitive Proxy Statement (the "Proxy Statement") for its 2026 Annual Meeting. The full text of the A&R 2024 Plan was included as Appendix A to the Proxy Statement, which was filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission on January 22, 2026 and is incorporated herein by reference.

The full text of this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/46765/000004676526000017/hp-20260304.htm

Any exhibits and associated documents for this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/46765/000004676526000017/0000046765-26-000017-index.htm

Public companies must file a Form 8-K, or current report, with the SEC generally within four days of any event that could materially affect a company's financial position or the value of its shares.

data.title: Teva Pharmaceutical Executives Sell Shares
data.description:
Daniell Richard, EVP, Europe Commercial at Teva Pharmaceutical Industries Ltd, sold 91,345 ordinary shares on March 4, 2026, under a Rule 10b5-1 trading plan. The transactions were executed at weighted average prices of $32.4645 and $32.9474, resulting in a total sale amount of $2,983,516. Following these transactions, Richard directly owns 85,755 ordinary shares of the company.

RelationshipEVP, Europe Commercial
TypeSell
